UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile within the library system, storing non-authentication related information.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

email.description: User's email address, used for communication (not authentication).

libraryCardNumber.description: Unique identifier for the user's library card.

membershipStatus.description: The current status of the user's library membership (e.g., 'active', 'suspended', 'expired').

joinedDate.description: The date and time when the user joined the library.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Author entity.

name.description: The full name of the author.

biography.description: A brief biography or description of the author.

dateOfDeath.description: The author's date of death, if applicable.

Genre.title: Genre
Genre.description: Represents a category or genre for books.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Genre entity.

name.description: The name of the genre (e.g., 'Fantasy', 'Science Fiction', 'History').

description.description: A brief description of the genre.

Book.title: Book
Book.description: Represents a book available in the library.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Book entity.

isbn.description: The International Standard Book Number (ISBN), a unique commercial book identifier.

publicationYear.description: The year the book was published.

description.description: A summary or synopsis of the book.

availableCopies.description: The number of copies currently available for loan.

totalCopies.description: The total number of copies of this book owned by the library.

authorId.description: Reference to the Author of the book. (Relationship: Author 1:N Book)

genreIds.description: References to Genres associated with this book. (Relationship: Genre N:N Book)

Loan.title: Loan
Loan.description: Represents an instance of a book being borrowed by a user.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Loan entity.

bookId.description: Reference to the Book that was loaned. (Relationship: Book 1:N Loan)

userProfileId.description: Reference to the UserProfile who borrowed the book. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Loan)

loanDate.description: The date and time the book was borrowed.

dueDate.description: The date and time the book is due to be returned.

returnDate.description: The actual date and time the book was returned, if applicable.

status.description: The current status of the loan (e.g., 'borrowed', 'returned', 'overdue').

definition.description: Stores user-specific profile information. The `userProfileId` directly corresponds to the Firebase Auth UID (`request.auth.uid`), providing denormalized ownership for authorization independence.

params.name: userProfileId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user profile, matching the Firebase Auth UID.

definition.description: Contains public information about authors. Accessible for read by all authenticated users. Write access is restricted to administrative roles.

params.name: authorId
params.description: The unique identifier of the author.

definition.description: Contains public information about book genres. Accessible for read by all authenticated users. Write access is restricted to administrative roles.

params.name: genreId
params.description: The unique identifier of the genre.

definition.description: Contains public information about books available in the library. Accessible for read by all authenticated users. Write access is restricted to administrative roles.

params.name: bookId
params.description: The unique identifier of the book.

definition.description: Stores individual loan records, nested under the user's path. The `userId` in the path (which must match `request.auth.uid`) provides denormalized ownership for authorization independence. The `userProfileId` field within the document should match the `userId` from the path.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who borrowed the book, matching their Firebase Auth UID.

params.name: loanId
params.description: The unique identifier for the loan record.

firestore.reasoning:
This Firestore data structure is designed with a strong emphasis on security, scalability, and debuggability, adhering to the core design principles provided. It utilizes path-based authorization, structural segregation, and denormalization to ensure robust security rules and efficient data access.

Authorization Independence & Denormalization:
1.  UserProfile Data (`/user_profiles/{userProfileId}`): Each user's profile is stored in a top-level collection, with the document ID (`userProfileId`) directly matching the user's Firebase Authentication UID (`request.auth.uid`). This denormalizes ownership directly into the document path, making authorization rules atomic (e.g., `allow read, update, delete: if request.auth.uid == userProfileId;`). No `get()` calls to parent documents are necessary for profile access control.
2.  Loan Data (`/users/{userId}/loans/{loanId}`): Loan records are nested under a user-specific path. The `userId` wildcard in the path *is* the primary denormalized authorization context, which must match `request.auth.uid`. This explicitly links each loan to its owner at the path level. While the `Loan` entity schema also contains `userProfileId`, its presence in the path provides the necessary context for rules, eliminating cross-document `get()` dependencies for authorization. For instance, `allow read, create: if request.auth.uid == userId;` is self-contained.
3.  Public Data (`/authors`, `/genres`, `/books`): These collections hold publicly readable information about the library's content. Their access rules do not rely on `get()` calls for authorization. Write access to these collections would be restricted to administrative roles, ensuring data integrity without complex dependencies.

QAPs (Rules are not Filters):
1.  User-Specific Data (`UserProfile`, `Loan`): Path-based ownership naturally supports Queryable Access Patterns (QAPs). A user can easily retrieve their own `UserProfile` by knowing their `uid`. For `Loans`, a user can `list` all their loans by querying `/users/$(request.auth.uid)/loans`. Firestore rules can directly enforce `allow list: if request.auth.uid == userId;`, ensuring that the database only returns documents belonging to the requesting user, preventing the need for client-side filtering or inefficient server-side rule filtering.
2.  Public Data (`Authors`, `Genres`, `Books`): These collections are designed for universal read access. `list` operations will return all documents, allowing any user to browse the library's catalog. This fulfills the QAP without requiring any filtering based on user identity for read access.

Structural Segregation:
Data is segregated into collections with distinct security postures:
*   `/user_profiles`: For private, user-owned profile data.
*   `/users/{userId}/loans`: For private, user-owned loan records.
*   `/authors`, `/genres`, `/books`: For publicly browsable library content.

DBAC (Database-Backed Access Control):
For administrative roles (e.g., managing Authors, Genres, Books), the DBAC principle mandates roles be stored in the database. Ideally, this would be implemented by a dedicated collection like `/roles_admin/{userId}`, where the mere existence of a document for a `userId` grants administrative privileges. However, as the provided `backend.entities` schema does not include a 'Role' entity, a specific path for `roles_admin` cannot be included in the `firestorePaths` array while strictly adhering to the output schema's requirement for `definition.schema.$ref` to a defined entity. In a full implementation, a 'Role' entity would be defined, allowing for this critical segregation of authorization data and fulfilling the 'Existence over Content' mandate for roles.
